content: 8 A Machine You Can Teach in Your BrowserPicture this: you point your webcam at a coffee mug, click a button a few times, then hold up a notebook and click a few more. Thirty seconds later, your browser can tell the two apart ‚ÄĒ no code, no cloud, no PhD required. That‚Äôs Google‚Äôs Teachable Machine (TM). But here‚Äôs the wild part ‚ÄĒ that friendly little UI isn‚Äôt magic. Every button you press is quietly driving the entire TensorFlow.js engine underneath. In my last post, we met the four big ideas of TensorFlow.js: tensors, the APIs, the backends, and the model lifecycle. Today, let‚Äôs trace exactly what happens under the hood ‚ÄĒ step by step ‚ÄĒ as you teach a browser to recognize you holding a coffee mug versus a notebook. flowchart LR A["Webcam frames"] --> B["Tensors"] B --> C["APIsLayers + Ops"] C --> D["BackendsGPU / CPU"] D --> E["LifecycleExport model"]
title: Watching TensorFlow.js Come Alive --- A Peek Inside Teachable Machine

content: 9 Tensors ‚ÄĒ When You Hold Up the MugYou click ‚ÄúHold to Record‚ÄĚ under Class 1, and your webcam starts capturing a stream of raw image pixels. But here‚Äôs the catch: JavaScript can‚Äôt just hand a grid of pixels to a neural network and hope for the best. So TensorFlow.js steps in. It intercepts each webcam frame and reshapes that grid of red, green, and blue pixels into a tensor ‚ÄĒ a neat 3D (or 4D) box of numbers the network actually understands. And because tensors are immutable, every single frame you capture spins up a fresh tensor: A new frame means a new tensor ‚ÄĒ never an edit to an old one. Each of these tensors is shipped straight to your device‚Äôs GPU memory whenever available, so it can be crunched instantly ‚ÄĒ without freezing your browser tab.
title: Watching TensorFlow.js Come Alive --- A Peek Inside Teachable Machine

content: 10 The APIs ‚ÄĒ When You Hit ‚ÄúTrain Model‚ÄĚTeachable Machine doesn‚Äôt learn from scratch. It stands on the shoulders of a pre-trained base model (usually MobileNet) that already knows what lines, edges, and shapes look like. This is where TensorFlow.js‚Äôs two API layers team up. The Layers API ‚ÄĒ the blueprint. When the page loads, it sketches out the network‚Äôs high-level design. In plain words, it tells TensorFlow.js: ‚ÄúKeep all of MobileNet‚Äôs early layers frozen ‚ÄĒ they already understand shapes. Just add a fresh, empty classification layer at the end for Mug vs. Notebook.‚ÄĚ The Ops API ‚ÄĒ the math. The moment you hit Train Model, the eager execution engine wakes up. The Ops API fires off rapid-fire math behind the scenes ‚ÄĒ matrix multiplications (tf.matMul), weight adjustments, epoch after epoch ‚ÄĒ until the network finally learns to tell your two objects apart. flowchart TB subgraph MN["Pre-trained MobileNet"] F["Early layers ‚ÄĒ FROZENalready know edges & shapes"] end F --> N["New classification layerMug vs. Notebook"] L["Layers APIthe blueprint"] -. "builds" .-> N O["Ops APIthe math ‚ÄĒ tf.matMul"] -. "trains" .-> N
title: Watching TensorFlow.js Come Alive --- A Peek Inside Teachable Machine

content: 11 Backends ‚ÄĒ Why Your Fan Might Start SpinningA little loading wheel appears. Training has begun ‚ÄĒ and somewhere, your laptop fan might quietly kick in. Choosing the engine. Before the very first calculation runs, TensorFlow.js takes a quick look around your browser. Spots a modern setup with hardware acceleration? It hands the heavy math straight to the WebGPU or WebGL backend. The fallback plan. That choice is made once, at startup ‚ÄĒ not quietly swapped halfway through. So if you‚Äôre on an older tablet or a locked-down laptop where the GPU is off-limits, TensorFlow.js simply starts on another registered backend, like WASM or plain CPU. The result is the same either way: The training still finishes ‚ÄĒ it just takes a little longer. flowchart TB A["TensorFlow.js starts up"] --> B{"Hardwareacceleration?"} B -- "Yes" --> C["WebGPU / WebGL backend"] B -- "No" --> D["WASM / CPU backend"] C --> E["Training finishes ‚ÄĒ fast"] D --> F["Training finishes ‚ÄĒ just slower"]
title: Watching TensorFlow.js Come Alive --- A Peek Inside Teachable Machine

content: 12 Lifecycle ‚ÄĒ When You Click ‚ÄúExport Model‚ÄĚThe bar hits 100%. Just like that, you‚Äôve built a working, custom brain ‚ÄĒ living entirely inside a browser tab. Saving the state. Choose to download or upload, and the lifecycle loop packages your freshly tuned weights into small binary array files, bundled alongside a model.json that maps out the network‚Äôs structure. Closing the loop. Now you can drop those files anywhere ‚ÄĒ a totally separate web project, a Node.js server, even a hardware device. Because the architecture stays identical, your exported model spins right back up on whatever device your user is holding, ready to read new tensors all over again.
title: Watching TensorFlow.js Come Alive --- A Peek Inside Teachable Machine

content: 13 From a Coffee Mug to the Bigger PictureSo the next time you casually train Teachable Machine to spot your mug, remember what‚Äôs really going on: pixels becoming tensors, a frozen giant lending its knowledge, an engine picking the fastest path it can find, and a tidy little brain packed up to travel the world. The same four ideas ‚ÄĒ tensors, APIs, backends, lifecycle ‚ÄĒ power everything from a webcam to the serious machine learning running across the web. One you can build in thirty seconds, and one that‚Äôs quietly redefining how the world is recognized. Cheers!

content: Wait‚Ä¶ a web page can run AI? Yep. Thanks to TensorFlow.js, your browser isn‚Äôt just for reading articles, watching videos, or endlessly scrolling social media. It can load neural networks, process webcam frames, recognize speech, and make predictions locally in real time‚ÄĒ feeling a bit like giving your browser a brain. But how does it actually pull this off? Let‚Äôs take a look under the hood.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 4 Four big ideas, one clever libraryEverything TensorFlow.js does hang off four core concepts. Get these, and the rest of the architecture snaps into focus.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 4.1 Idea 1 ‚ÄĒ Tensors: how TensorFlow.js represents dataThink of tensors as the universal language of TensorFlow.js. A number is a tensor. A list is a tensor. An image? A tensor with more dimensions. Three things make them special: They‚Äôre immutable ‚ÄĒ every operation creates a new tensor rather than modifying the old one. They can live in GPU memory, which is how the fast backends get their speed. And they scale in dimensions, from a single value all the way up to batches of images. Same idea, more dimensions: from a single row of numbers to a whole batch of images.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 4.2 Idea 2 ‚ÄĒ APIs: how TensorFlow.js defines the computationHow do you tell a tensor what to do? TensorFlow.js gives you two levels of vocabulary. The Ops API contains the verbs of numerical computing: add, multiply, reshape. The Layers API, modeled closely on Keras, combines those operations into larger building blocks for neural networks. Use Ops when you want the individual moves; use Layers when you want to assemble the model. TensorFlow.js supports eager execution: call an operation and the computation starts immediately. You can inspect results as you go, using ordinary JavaScript loops and conditions. The API describes what should happen, but says nothing about the machine that will make it happen. So where actually performs the calculation? Two levels of vocabulary, one computation underneath.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 4.3 Idea 3 ‚ÄĒ Backends: where the computation runsHere‚Äôs the genuinely clever part. When your code says ‚Äúmultiply these matrices,‚ÄĚ TensorFlow.js doesn‚Äôt have one place to do that ‚ÄĒ it has five. A backend selector inspects the environment and routes every operation to the fastest engine available, falling back gracefully when fancy hardware isn‚Äôt around. Write the model once. The selector picks the engine at runtime. Backend Think of it as Best for WebGPU Modern GPU compute, no disguises The fastest option, where supported WebGL A GPU smuggled in via graphics shaders Most browsers today WASM A supercharged CPU Small models, wide compatibility CPU The universal fallback Compatibility, always works Node.js Native TensorFlow, no browser needed Servers
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 4.4 Idea 4 ‚ÄĒ Lifecycle: a loop, not just a lineFrame it as: you‚Äôd expect a lifecycle to be a straight line ‚ÄĒ train, convert, deploy, done. TensorFlow.js makes it a loop. Deployment isn‚Äôt the end, because the deployed model can keep training on live user data, save its improved self to browser storage, and pick up where it left off next visit. The point of the section becomes: the browser isn‚Äôt just the destination, it‚Äôs also a training ground.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 5 Easter egg: where Teachable Machine fitsGoogle‚Äôs Teachable Machine (TM) is where these four ideas really come alive. Every click, class, and training run sets the TensorFlow.js engine whirring behind the scenes. So, how does that actually work under the hood of TM? Stay tuned for the next post! Cheers!

content: 1 The Secret Behind a Folded PizzaPicture this: today is pizza night! You grab a fresh, cheesy slice of pizza. It‚Äôs hot, it‚Äôs gooey‚Ä¶ and it immediately flops over like it‚Äôs given up on life. So, what do you do? You pull the classic move ‚ÄĒ you fold the slice. Just a quick bend along the crust toward the center, and magically, the floppy slice becomes firm and easy to hold! But here‚Äôs the wild part ‚ÄĒ that little fold isn‚Äôt just a clever eating hack. It also hides a beautiful piece of geometry ‚ÄĒ Gaussian curvature.
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚ÄĚ--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 2 Gaussian CurvaturePicture that slice of pizza in your hand. Before you fold it, it‚Äôs just a flat piece of dough ‚ÄĒ smooth, even, and perfectly level. Every point on it is flat, meaning its curvature is zero. In mathematics, we describe how a surface bends by looking at two special directions at any point: the maximum value of curvature in one direction ‚ÄĒ that‚Äôs $k_1$. the minimum value of curvature in the other direction ‚ÄĒ that‚Äôs $k_2$. Multiply those two numbers, and you get the Gaussian curvature: K=k1‚čÖk2.K = k_1 \cdot k_2.K=k1‚Äč‚čÖk2‚Äč.And just like that, different surfaces reveal their own ‚Äúbending personalities‚ÄĚ. Before you fold the pizza, it‚Äôs a flat surface ‚ÄĒ which means K=k1√ók2=0√ó0=0.K = k_1 \times k_2 = 0 \times 0 = 0.K=k1‚Äč√ók2‚Äč=0√ó0=0.And here‚Äôs the beautiful part: according to Gauss‚Äôs Remarkable Theorem, The Gaussian curvature of a surface does not change if you bend the surface without stretching it. In other words ‚ÄĒ folding the pizza doesn‚Äôt change its Gaussian curvature. That is, even after the fold, it still belongs to the geometric world of plane.
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚ÄĚ--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 3 Why Does the Pizza Hold Up?Why does a folded pizza suddenly become strong enough to hold all that gooey cheese and sauce? From a mathematical point of view, the pizza‚Äôs Gaussian curvature $K=0 $, both before and after you fold it. Then when you fold the slice along one direction, turning it into a neat little ‚ÄúV‚ÄĚ shape, something interesting happens: In the direction across the fold, the pizza bends, which means $k_1 \neq 0$. Hence, along the fold curve itself, it must be $k_2 = 0$, meaning, that fold curve is a straight line, staying perfectly flat. In other words, folding the slice greatly increases its stiffness along that fold line. Once folded, the pizza can‚Äôt bend easily in that direction‚ÄĒ that‚Äôs why it suddenly gains strength, feels firm, steady, and ready to handle all that cheesy weight.
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚ÄĚ--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 4 Developable vs. Non-developable SurfacesIn math, if a smooth surface has Gaussian curvature K = 0 everywhere, we call it a developable surface. In simple terms, it‚Äôs the kind of surface you could flatten perfectly onto a sheet of paper without stretching or tearing. Think of: a folded pizza slice, a roll of toilet paper, or an ice-cream cone By contrast, if a surface has places where its Gaussian curvature isn‚Äôt zero ($K \neq 0$), it‚Äôs called a non-developable surface. That means it refuses to lie flat ‚ÄĒ you can‚Äôt turn it into a plane without stretching, squeezing, or ripping it apart, no matter how hard you try. Orange peel: it curves in every direction ($K > 0$), which is why it can never lie flat. Potato chip: one side curves up, the other curves down ($K < 0$); try to flatten it, and it‚Äôll snap. This also explains why a perfectly distortion-free world map is impossible. The Earth‚Äôs surface is curved ‚ÄĒ it has positive Gaussian curvature ‚ÄĒ while paper has zero curvature. And by Gauss‚Äôs Remarkable Theorem, bending without stretching doesn‚Äôt change curvature. So when we try to project our round planet onto a flat map, something has to give ‚ÄĒ area, shape, or distance will always end up distorted. Not a design flaw ‚ÄĒ just mathematics doing its thing!
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚ÄĚ--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 5 From Pizza to the CosmosGaussian curvature isn‚Äôt just a neat geometric curiosity ‚ÄĒ it‚Äôs one of the cornerstones of modern physicsÔľĀ A century after Gauss, Albert Einstein took this elegant idea and used it to build General Relativity. In Einstein‚Äôs universe, gravity isn‚Äôt some invisible force tugging at planets and stars ‚ÄĒ it‚Äôs the curvature of spacetime itself.
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚ÄĚ--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 1 Story Time TwistThe other night, I was reading The Tortoise and the Hare with my kid. You know the ending ‚ÄĒ the rabbit takes a nap, the tortoise keeps going, and‚Ä¶ yep, the tortoise wins. Classic. But when we finished the story ‚ÄĒ yeah, you caught me ‚ÄĒ I just couldn‚Äôt resist adding a twist: " What if the very next day‚Ä¶ they race again?" This time, the rabbit wants to look ‚Äúfair.‚ÄĚ He smirks and says: ‚ÄúTortoise, you‚Äôre way too slow. To make things exciting, I‚Äôll give you a 100-meter head start!‚ÄĚ The race begins. Whoooosh! The rabbit blasts off and quickly covers that 100 meters. But when he hits the tortoise‚Äôs starting line ‚ÄĒ guess what? the tortoise has crawled 10 meters farther. The rabbit keeps running fast and quickly caught up the 10 meters ‚Ä¶ only to see the tortoise has moved ahead by 1 more meter. The rabbit tries again. He runs that 1 meter in almost no time‚Ä¶ but the tortoise has gone 0.1 meter farther‚Ä¶ And so it goes ‚ÄĒ every time the rabbit gets close, the tortoise is just a little farther ahead! At this point, the kid‚Äôs eyes went wide: ‚ÄúWait, what? Does that mean the rabbit can never catch the tortoise?‚ÄĚ Is that possible? Or is there a deeper trick hiding here?
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 2 Intuitive ExplanationI gave a little mysterious smile, ‚ÄúHey, remember when you raced with your friend Andy last week? He dashed ahead quite a long way while you weren‚Äôt looking. And‚Ä¶ what happened then?‚ÄĚ My kid jumped in right away, full of confidence: ‚ÄúI caught him! Because I was much faster ‚ÄĒ and I even passed him!‚ÄĚ Then he slapped his forehead like he‚Äôd just solved the biggest puzzle: ‚ÄúOHHHH‚ÄĒso the rabbit can catch the tortoise after all!‚ÄĚ I nodded, with a gentle grin: " Yep! The faster one always catches the slower one in an amount of time‚Ä¶ even if it feels like you‚Äôre chasing again, and again, and again."
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 3 The ‚ÄúTrap‚ÄĚ of Zeno PhenomenonIn the story, every time the rabbit gets to where the tortoise was, the tortoise has already moved a tiny bit ahead. It feels like the rabbit is always just a little too late, and can never catch up! This is the very ‚Äôtrap‚Äô of Zeno‚Äôs paradox: mixing up infinitely many chasing steps with an infinitely long amount of time. Up next, let‚Äôs bring in the idea of limits to reveal the surprise: even with infinitely many steps, the total time is actually finite! Alight, fun time for us big kids ‚Äď ready, go!
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 4 Math Explanation
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 4.1 SetupTortoise has a head start distance $D$. Tortoise speed: $v_T$. Rabbit speed: $v_A$, with $v_A > v_T$. We break the chase into infinitely many segments: each time the rabbit runs to where the tortoise was, the tortoise moves further.
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 4.2 Distances / gapsLet $d_1 = D.$ Time taken by Achilles to cover $d_1$ is t1=d1vA=DvA.t_1 = \frac{d_1}{v_A} = \frac{D}{v_A}.t1‚Äč=vA‚Äčd1‚Äč‚Äč=vA‚ÄčD‚Äč. During that time, tortoise advances: őĒ1=t1‚čÖvT=DvA‚čÖvT.\Delta_1 = t_1 \cdot v_T = \frac{D}{v_A} \cdot v_T.őĒ1‚Äč=t1‚Äč‚čÖvT‚Äč=vA‚ÄčD‚Äč‚čÖvT‚Äč. Thus the remaining gap this time becomes d2=őĒ1.d_2 = \Delta_1 .d2‚Äč=őĒ1‚Äč. By the same logic, t2=d2vA=DvA‚čÖvTvA. t_2 = \frac{d_2}{v_A} = \frac{D}{v_A} \cdot \frac{v_T}{v_A}.t2‚Äč=vA‚Äčd2‚Äč‚Äč=vA‚ÄčD‚Äč‚čÖvA‚ÄčvT‚Äč‚Äč. Then the tortoise advances again, gap shrinks, etc. We get the general term tn=dnvA=DvA(vTvA)‚ÄČn‚ąí1. \quad t_n = \frac{d_n}{v_A} = \frac{D}{v_A} \left(\frac{v_T}{v_A}\right)^{\,n-1}.tn‚Äč=vA‚Äčdn‚Äč‚Äč=vA‚ÄčD‚Äč(vA‚ÄčvT‚Äč‚Äč)n‚ąí1.
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 4.3 Time sum (infinite series)So the total time Achilles needs to catch the tortoise is T=‚ąĎn=1‚ąětn=DvA‚ąĎn=0‚ąě(vTvA)n. T = \sum_{n=1}^\infty t_n = \frac{D}{v_A} \sum_{n=0}^\infty \left(\frac{v_T}{v_A}\right)^n.T=n=1‚ąĎ‚ąě‚Äčtn‚Äč=vA‚ÄčD‚Äčn=0‚ąĎ‚ąě‚Äč(vA‚ÄčvT‚Äč‚Äč)n.Since $\tfrac{v_T}{v_A} < 1$,the series converges (geometric series): ‚ąĎn=0‚ąě(vTvA)n=11‚ąívTvA=vAvA‚ąívT.\sum_{n=0}^\infty \left(\frac{v_T}{v_A}\right)^n = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{v_T}{v_A}} = \frac{v_A}{v_A - v_T}.n=0‚ąĎ‚ąě‚Äč(vA‚ÄčvT‚Äč‚Äč)n=1‚ąívA‚ÄčvT‚Äč‚Äč1‚Äč=vA‚Äč‚ąívT‚ÄčvA‚Äč‚Äč. Hence T=DvA‚čÖvAvA‚ąívT=DvA‚ąívT.T = \frac{D}{v_A} \cdot \frac{v_A}{v_A - v_T} = \frac{D}{v_A - v_T}.T=vA‚ÄčD‚Äč‚čÖvA‚Äč‚ąívT‚ÄčvA‚Äč‚Äč=vA‚Äč‚ąívT‚ÄčD‚Äč.That is a finite positive time. After that time $T$, the rabbit catches up.
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 5 ClosingJust like the race between the Tortoise and the Hare, or that treasure hunt we talked about earlier with steady speed, the idea of infinitely many events fitting into a limited time is what we call a Zeno behavior. Think of dropping a little ball: Boing! it bounces high the first time, then lower, then even lower‚Ä¶ The trick to handling problems like these is: instead of getting stuck on the thought of ‚Äėforever‚Äô, we assume a definite stopping time, and then calculate the final result after all those infinitely tiny bounces.‚ÄĚ Bam! >_<
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 1 Story Time Twist:Hey friends, remember our last treasure hunting story? Leo had to move in infinite steps within a limited time, but since he was walking at a steady speed, he eventually reached the treasure. Cool, right? But‚Ä¶ what if we change the rules a little bit, like the daily speed is decreasing? Imagine this: the first day, he walks half of the distance, that is $1/2$. The second day, he walks half of what‚Äôs left, which is $1/4$. The third day, he goes half of the remainder, which is $1/8$. And it keeps going, and going, and going‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ So, will Leo ever reach the treasure? Well, here‚Äôs the twist: the answer is‚Ä¶ NOPE! Why? Let‚Äôs dive in and find out!‚ÄĚ
title: Zhuangzi's Stick

content: 1 Story Time Twist:A few days ago, while reading Pedro‚Äôs Pirate Treasure to my kid, I made up a new puzzle story: Imagine you are the little pirate, Leo. One day, you spot a brand-new mark on the treasure map! Excited, you set off on your adventure. But oh no‚ÄĒthe map has a strange spell written on it: ‚ÄėTo reach the treasure, you can only walk half of the distance left each time. No faster, no slower.‚Äô So, the first time, you walk half of the distance, that is $1/2$. The second time, you walk half of what‚Äôs left, which is $1/4$. The third time, you go half of the remainder, which is $1/8$. And it keeps going, and going, and going‚Ä¶" The kid shouted: ‚ÄúOh no! This will never end! Does that mean I can never get there?‚ÄĚ I smiled and said: ‚ÄúNot true! Imagine this: if it takes you one whole day to walk the first half, then the rest‚ÄĒno matter how many tiny steps it‚Äôs split into‚ÄĒonly takes one more day. So, in just two days, boom‚ÄĒyou‚Äôve got the treasure!‚ÄĚ The kid: ‚ÄúOHHHH, haha, I get it!‚ÄĚ Now it‚Äôs fun time for us big kids! @_@ Let‚Äôs see the math explanation.
title: Treasure Hunting ---Zeno Phenomenon (1)

content: 2 Math Explanation:The trick isn‚Äôt only about ‚ÄúCan you get there?‚ÄĚ ‚ÄĒ it‚Äôs really about ‚ÄúCan you make it in a limited amount of time?‚ÄĚ After all, nobody in real life keeps walking forever! That‚Äôs why looking at the time makes more sense than just adding up the distance.
title: Treasure Hunting ---Zeno Phenomenon (1)

content: 2.1 Time Calculation:Let the total distance from $A$ to $B$ be $D$. Assume motion is at constant speed $v$. We may slice the journey into infinitely many segments: first cover $D/2$, then $D/4$, then $D/8$ ‚Ä¶ Let the distance of the $n-th$ segment be: dn=D2n,n=1,2,3,‚Ä¶d_n = \frac{D}{2^n}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3,\dotsdn‚Äč=2nD‚Äč,n=1,2,3,‚Ä¶The total time after n segments is Tn=‚ąĎk=1ntk=1v‚ąĎk=1ndk=1v‚ąĎk=1nD2k=1vSn. T_n = \sum_{k=1}^n t_k = \frac{1}{v} \sum_{k=1}^n d_k = \frac{1}{v} \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{D}{2^k} =\frac{1}{v} S_n. Tn‚Äč=k=1‚ąĎn‚Äčtk‚Äč=v1‚Äčk=1‚ąĎn‚Äčdk‚Äč=v1‚Äčk=1‚ąĎn‚Äč2kD‚Äč=v1‚ÄčSn‚Äč. Thus lim‚Ā°n‚Üí‚ąěTn=1vlim‚Ā°n‚Üí‚ąěSn=Dvlim‚Ā°n‚Üí‚ąě‚ąĎk=1n12k=Dv.\lim_{n\to\infty} T_n = \frac{1}{v} \lim_{n\to\infty} S_n = \frac{D}{v}\lim_{n\to\infty} \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{1}{2^k} = \frac{D}{v}.n‚Üí‚ąělim‚ÄčTn‚Äč=v1‚Äčn‚Üí‚ąělim‚ÄčSn‚Äč=vD‚Äčn‚Üí‚ąělim‚Äčk=1‚ąĎn‚Äč2k1‚Äč=vD‚Äč.Hence even though you ‚Äúdivide into infinitely many times or steps,‚ÄĚ the total time is finite, namely $D/v$. You arrive at B at time $T = D/v$.
title: Treasure Hunting ---Zeno Phenomenon (1)

content: 2.2 TipsThus the paradox is resolved: infinite subdivision does not force infinite time, because each successive distance moved forward gets smaller and smaller, making the time contributions shrink to zero. Bingo! ^_^
title: Treasure Hunting ---Zeno Phenomenon (1)

content: 3 Follow-UpZhuangzi‚Äôs Stick Zhuangzi once said: ‚ÄúA foot-long stick, if halved each day, will never be used up.‚ÄĚ This expresses the idea of infinite divisibility‚ÄĒno matter how many times you cut it in half, there is always something left. Is that similar to our ‚Äútreasure hunting‚ÄĚ, or any contradiction between the two stories? The story is continuing ‚ÄĒ let‚Äôs stay tuned! ^_^

content: 1 Question:Evaluate ‚ąęCxx2+y2dy‚ąíyx2+y2dx\int_C\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx‚ąęC‚Äčx2+y2x‚Äčdy‚ąíx2+y2y‚Äčdx where $C$ is the rectangular curve with vertices $(2,3)$,$(-2,3)$,$(-2,-3)$,$(2,-3)$, taken in the anti-clockwise direction.
title: Similar example for Discussion of Green's Theorem (4)

content: 2 Discussion:
title: Similar example for Discussion of Green's Theorem (4)

content: 2.1 Idea 1:The curve $C$ consists of $C_1 :y=3, 2\leq x \leq -2$,$C_2 :x=-2, 3\leq y \leq -3$, $C_3 :y=3, -2\leq x \leq 2$,$C_4 :x=2, -3\leq y \leq 3$, and the diagram is given as below: Hence, $$\begin{aligned}& \quad \int_C\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx \\& =\int_{C_1+C_2+C_3+C_4}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx \\& =\int_{C_1}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx + \int_{C_2}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx \\& \quad + \int_{C_3}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx + \int_{C_4}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx \\& =\int_{2}^{-2}-\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\;\int_{3}^{-3}\frac{-2}{4+y^2}dy \;+\;\int_{-2}^{2}-\frac{-3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\; \int_{-3}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \\& =\int_{-2}^{2}\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\;\int_{-3}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \;+\;\int_{-2}^{2}\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\; \int_{-3}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \\& =2 \Big(\int_{-2}^{2}\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\;\int_{-3}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \Big) \\& =4 \Big(\int_{0}^{2}\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\;\int_{0}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \Big) \\& =4 \Big(\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2} \frac{1} {(\frac{x}{3})^2+1}dx + \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{3}\frac{1}{1+(\frac{y}{2})^2}dy\Big) \\& =4 \Big(\int_{0}^{2}\frac{1}{(\frac{x}{3})^2+1}d\Big(\frac{x}{3}\Big) + \int_{0}^{3}\frac{1}{1+(\frac{y}{2})^2}d\Big(\frac{y}{2}\Big) \Big) \\& =4\Big(\arctan\Big(\frac{x}{3}\Big)\Big|_{x=0}^{x=2} + \arctan\Big(\frac{y}{2}\Big)\Big|_{y=0}^{y=3}\Big) \\& =4 \Big(\arctan\Big(\frac{2}{3}\Big) + \arctan\Big(\frac{3}{2}\Big)\Big) \\& =4 \Big(\frac{\pi}{2}\Big)=2{\pi}\end{aligned}$$Idea 2 & 3 The corresponding domain $D$ enclosed by $C$ is given as below: Next, $$\int_C\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx=\int_C\begin{pmatrix}\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}\\\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\end{pmatrix}\cdot\begin{pmatrix}dx\\dy\end{pmatrix}=\int_C F \cdot dr,$$where $F=\begin{pmatrix}\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}\\ \frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}P\\ Q\end{pmatrix}$, $dr=\begin{pmatrix} dx\ dy\end{pmatrix}$, and $r=r(t)$ is the parametric equation of the curve $C$. Note that $F$ is undefined at $(x,y)=(0,0)$.
title: Similar example for Discussion of Green's Theorem (4)

content: 2.2 Idea 2:Assume $ F=\begin{pmatrix}P\\ Q\end{pmatrix}$. We know $F$ is undefined at $O(0,0)$, and then it‚Äôs easy to check $P_y=Q_x$ when $(x,y)$ is on the domain $D$ except the origin $O(0,0)$. Thus, by the test for the conservative vector field, $F$ is conservative on $D$ except $(0,0)$, NOT on the whole region $D$ or for the each point of region $D$. Hence, Fundamental Theorem of Line integrals is NOT applicable here, which is, we can not perform $$\int_C F \cdot dr = f(r(b))-f(r(a)).$$
title: Similar example for Discussion of Green's Theorem (4)

content: 2.3 Idea 3:As $F$ is undefined at $O(0,0)$,the actual domain of integration in this problem should be $$D^*=\{(x,y): D, \text{and } (x,y) \neq (0,0)\}, $$which is a rectangular region with a hole,and we call it non-closed. Hence, Green‚Äôs Theorem can NOT be applied directly to solve this kind of problem. So what else shall we do? We can draw a unit circle, $i.e. C_2:x^2+y^2=1$, where the little hole $O(0,0)$ is within this circle, as shown in the following diagram: Where R is is the region between $C$ and $C_1$, and the boundary of $R$ is $\partial R=C-C_1$ By the Green‚Äôs Theorem, $$\oint_{\partial R} F \cdot dr =\iint_R (Q_x-P_y)dx =0, $$as $P_y=Q_x$ is conservative on the region $R$. $\implies \int_{C_-C_1} F \cdot dr =0$, $\implies \int_{C}F \cdot dr =\int_{C_1}F \cdot dr=2\pi$,based on the result obtained in the remark below . 2.3.1 Remark:(1) Recall, for a vector field $F$, the line integral of $F$ along $C$ is $$\int_C F \cdot dr =\int_a^b F(r(t)) \cdot r'(t)\, dt.$$For $C_1$ in this problem, $r(t)=\begin{pmatrix}\cos t\\ \sin t\end{pmatrix}, 0 \leq t \leq 2\pi$, and hence, $$\int_C\frac{xdy-ydx}{(x^2+y^2)}=\int_C F \cdot dr =\int_0^{2\pi} \begin{pmatrix} -\sin t\\\ \cos t\end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} -\sin t\\\ \cos t\end{pmatrix}\,dt =2 \pi,$$(2) One of the requirement for Green‚Äôs Theorem is the domain must be closed, for example,

content: 1 QuestionThe temperature at a point $(x,y)$ on a metallic floor is $T(x,y)=4x^2-4xy+y^2$. Wearing thermocouples shoes that measures temperature, you walk along a circle of radius $5$ centered at the origin on the metallic floor. What are the highest and lowest temperatures measured by your shoes?
title: One Exercise about Lagrange Multiplier

content: 2 Solution
title: One Exercise about Lagrange Multiplier

content: 2.1 Lagrange MultiplierThe target: $T(x,y)=4x^2-4xy+y^2\Rightarrow T_x=8x-4y, T_y=-4x+2y$. The constraint: $g(x,y)=x^2+y^2-25=0\Rightarrow g_x=2x, g_y=2y$. By the method of Lagrange Multiplier, {8x‚ąí4y=őĽ‚čÖ2x¬†‚ąí4x+2y=őĽ‚čÖ2y¬†x2+y2=25‚áí{4x‚ąí2y=őĽ‚čÖx(1)¬†4x‚ąí2y=őĽ‚čÖ(‚ąí2y)(2)¬†x2+y2=25(3) \begin{cases} 8x-4y=\lambda\cdot 2x\\\ -4x+2y=\lambda\cdot 2y\\\ x^2+y^2=25 \end{cases} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} 4x-2y=\lambda\cdot x\quad (1)\\\ 4x-2y=\lambda\cdot (-2y)\quad (2)\\\ x^2+y^2=25 \quad (3) \end{cases} ‚é©‚é®‚éß‚Äč8x‚ąí4y=őĽ‚čÖ2x¬†‚ąí4x+2y=őĽ‚čÖ2y¬†x2+y2=25‚Äč‚áí‚é©‚é®‚éß‚Äč4x‚ąí2y=őĽ‚čÖx(1)¬†4x‚ąí2y=őĽ‚čÖ(‚ąí2y)(2)¬†x2+y2=25(3)‚Äč Then, (1)+(2)‚áíőĽ‚čÖx=őĽ‚čÖ(‚ąí2y)‚áíőĽ‚čÖ(x‚ąí2y)=0‚áíőĽ=0¬†or¬†x=‚ąí2y. (1)+(2)\Rightarrow \lambda\cdot x=\lambda\cdot (-2y)\Rightarrow \lambda\cdot (x-2y)=0\Rightarrow \lambda=0\text{ or } x=-2y. (1)+(2)‚áíőĽ‚čÖx=őĽ‚čÖ(‚ąí2y)‚áíőĽ‚čÖ(x‚ąí2y)=0‚áíőĽ=0¬†or¬†x=‚ąí2y. Sub $\lambda=0$ into (1), $4x-2y=0 \Rightarrow y=2x$. Putting them together, $x=-2y$ or $y=2x$. 2.1.1 Method 1Case 1. Sub $x=-2y$ into $(3) \Rightarrow (-2y)^2+y^2=25 \Rightarrow 5y^2=25\Rightarrow y^2=5$. {x=‚ąí25¬†y=5or{x=25¬†y=‚ąí5 \begin{cases} x=-2\sqrt{5}\\\ y=\sqrt{5} \end{cases} \quad \text{or} \quad \begin{cases} x=2\sqrt{5}\\\ y=-\sqrt{5} \end{cases} {x=‚ąí25‚Äč¬†y=5‚Äč‚Äčor{x=25‚Äč¬†y=‚ąí5‚Äč‚Äčcase 2. Sub $y=2x$ into $(3) \Rightarrow x^{2}+(2 x)^{2}=25 \Rightarrow 5 x^{2}=25 \Rightarrow x^{2}=5$. {x=5¬†y=25or{x=‚ąí5¬†y=‚ąí25 \begin{cases} x=\sqrt{5}\\\ y=2\sqrt{5} \end{cases} \quad \text{or} \quad \begin{cases} x=-\sqrt{5}\\\ y=-2\sqrt{5} \end{cases} {x=5‚Äč¬†y=25‚Äč‚Äčor{x=‚ąí5‚Äč¬†y=‚ąí25‚Äč‚Äč (1) Sub $x=-2 \sqrt{5}, y=\sqrt{5}$ into $T(x, y)$, T(x,y)=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2=(2x‚ąíy)2=(‚ąí45‚ąí5)2=(‚ąí55)2=25√ó5=125. T(x, y)=4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2}=(2 x-y)^{2}=(-4 \sqrt{5}-\sqrt{5})^{2}=(-5 \sqrt{5})^{2}=25 \times 5=125. T(x,y)=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2=(2x‚ąíy)2=(‚ąí45‚Äč‚ąí5‚Äč)2=(‚ąí55‚Äč)2=25√ó5=125. (2) Sub $x=2 \sqrt{5}, y=-\sqrt{5}$ into $T(x, y)$, T(x,y)=(2x‚ąíy)2=(45+5)2=(55)2=125. T(x, y)=(2 x-y)^{2}=(4 \sqrt{5}+\sqrt{5})^{2}=(5 \sqrt{5})^{2}=125. T(x,y)=(2x‚ąíy)2=(45‚Äč+5‚Äč)2=(55‚Äč)2=125. (3) Sub $x=\sqrt{5}, y=2 \sqrt{5}$ into $T(x, y)$, T(x,y)=(2x‚ąíy)2=0. T(x, y)=(2 x-y)^{2}=0. T(x,y)=(2x‚ąíy)2=0. (4) Sub $x=\sqrt{5}, y=-2 \sqrt{5}$ into $T(x, y)$ T(x,y)=(2x‚ąíy)2=0. T(x, y)=(2 x-y)^{2}=0. T(x,y)=(2x‚ąíy)2=0. Hence, the highest temperature is $125$, and the lowest one is $0$. 2.1.2 Method 2. ShortcutRecall: from the equation $(1)$ and $(2)$, we get $x=-2 y$ or $y=2 x$. Case 1. Sub $x=-2 y$ into (3), $(-2 y)^{2}+y^{2}=25 \Rightarrow y^{2}=5$ T(x,y)=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2¬†=(2x‚ąíy)2¬†=(2‚čÖ(‚ąí2y)‚ąíy)2¬†=25y2=25√ó5=125. \begin{aligned} T(x, y) &=4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2} \\\ &=(2 x-y)^{2} \\\ &=(2 \cdot(-2 y)-y)^{2} \\\ &=25 y^{2}=25 \times 5=125 . \end{aligned} T(x,y)¬†‚Äč=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2=(2x‚ąíy)2=(2‚čÖ(‚ąí2y)‚ąíy)2=25y2=25√ó5=125.‚ÄčCase2. Sub $y=2 x$ into (3), $x^{2}+(2 x)^{2}=25 \Rightarrow x^{2}=5$ T(x,y)=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2¬†=(2x‚ąíy)2¬†=(y‚ąíy)2¬†=0. \begin{aligned} T(x, y) &=4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2} \\\ &=(2 x-y)^{2} \\\ &=(y-y)^{2} \\\ &=0. \end{aligned} T(x,y)¬†‚Äč=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2=(2x‚ąíy)2=(y‚ąíy)2=0.‚ÄčHence, the highest temperature is $125$, and the lowest one is $0$. 2.1.3 RemarkResult 1.8A The maximum/minimum value of $f(x, y)$ subject to the constraint $g(x, y)=0$ occurs at a point $(x, y)$ that satisfies the following three equations fx=őĽgx¬†fy=őĽgy¬†g(x,y)=0 \begin{aligned} f_{x}&=\lambda g_{x} \\\ f_{y}&=\lambda g_{y} \\\ g(x, y)&=0 \end{aligned} fx‚Äč¬†fy‚Äč¬†g(x,y)‚Äč=őĽgx‚Äč=őĽgy‚Äč=0‚Äč for some constant $\lambda$, called a Lagrange multiplier.

T(x, y)=4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2}=(2 x-y)^{2}=(-4 \sqrt{5}-\sqrt{5})^{2}=(-5 \sqrt{5})^{2}=25 \times 5=125. T(x,y)=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2=(2x‚ąíy)2=(‚ąí45‚Äč‚ąí5‚Äč)2=(‚ąí55‚Äč)2=25√ó5=125. Sub $x=-2 y$ into (3), $(-2 y)^{2}+y^{2}=25 \Rightarrow y^{2}=5$ T(x,y)=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2¬†=(2x‚ąíy)2¬†=(2‚čÖ(‚ąí2y)‚ąíy)2¬†=25y2=25√ó5=125. \begin{aligned} T(x, y) &=4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2} \\\ &=(2 x-y)^{2} \\\ &=(2 \cdot(-2 y)-y)^{2} \\\ &=25 y^{2}=25 \times 5=125 . \end{aligned} T(x,y)¬†‚Äč=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2=(2x‚ąíy)2=(2‚čÖ(‚ąí2y)‚ąíy)2=25y2=25√ó5=125.‚ÄčCase2. Sub $y=2 x$ into (3), $x^{2}+(2 x)^{2}=25 \Rightarrow x^{2}=5$ T(x,y)=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2¬†=(2x‚ąíy)2¬†=(y‚ąíy)2¬†=0. \begin{aligned} T(x, y) &=4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2} \\\ &=(2 x-y)^{2} \\\ &=(y-y)^{2} \\\ &=0. \end{aligned} T(x,y)¬†‚Äč=4x2‚ąí4xy+y2=(2x‚ąíy)2=(y‚ąíy)2=0.‚ÄčHence, the highest temperature is $125$, and the lowest one is $0$. 2.1.3 RemarkResult 1.8A The maximum/minimum value of $f(x, y)$ subject to the constraint $g(x, y)=0$ occurs at a point $(x, y)$ that satisfies the following three equations fx=őĽgx¬†fy=őĽgy¬†g(x,y)=0 \begin{aligned} f_{x}&=\lambda g_{x} \\\ f_{y}&=\lambda g_{y} \\\ g(x, y)&=0 \end{aligned} fx‚Äč¬†fy‚Äč¬†g(x,y)‚Äč=őĽgx‚Äč=őĽgy‚Äč=0‚Äč for some constant $\lambda$, called a Lagrange multiplier.

content: 2.2 Parametric equationsThe target: $T(x, y)=4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2} \quad (1)$. The constraint: $g(x, y)=x^{2}+y^{2}-25=0 \quad (2)$. The parametric equation for (2) is {x=5cos‚Ā°őł¬†y=5sin‚Ā°őł \begin{cases} x=5 \cos \theta\\\ y=5 \sin \theta \end{cases} {x=5cosőł¬†y=5sinőł‚Äč and then sub into $(1)$, T(x,y)=(2x‚ąíy)2=(10cos‚Ā°őł‚ąí5sin‚Ā°őł)2¬†=(5‚čÖ(2cos‚Ā°őł‚ąísin‚Ā°őł))2¬†=25‚čÖ(2cos‚Ā°őł‚ąísin‚Ā°őł)2¬†=25‚čÖ(5‚čÖcos‚Ā°(őł+őĪ))2¬†=25‚čÖ5‚čÖcos‚Ā°2(őł+őĪ)¬†=125‚čÖcos‚Ā°2(őł+őĪ). \begin{aligned} T(x, y)=(2 x-y)^{2} &=(10 \cos \theta-5 \sin \theta)^{2} \\\ &=(5 \cdot(2 \cos \theta-\sin \theta))^{2}\\\ &=25 \cdot(2 \cos \theta-\sin \theta)^{2} \\\ &=25 \cdot(\sqrt{5} \cdot \cos (\theta+\alpha))^{2} \\\ &=25 \cdot 5 \cdot \cos ^{2}(\theta+\alpha)\\\ &=125 \cdot \cos ^{2}(\theta+\alpha). \end{aligned} T(x,y)=(2x‚ąíy)2¬†‚Äč=(10cosőł‚ąí5sinőł)2=(5‚čÖ(2cosőł‚ąísinőł))2=25‚čÖ(2cosőł‚ąísinőł)2=25‚čÖ(5‚Äč‚čÖcos(őł+őĪ))2=25‚čÖ5‚čÖcos2(őł+őĪ)=125‚čÖcos2(őł+őĪ).‚ÄčNote that $A\cos\theta-B\sin\theta=\sqrt{A^2+B^2}\cdot \cos (\theta+\alpha)$, where $\alpha=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)$. Hence $2\cos\theta-\sin\theta=\sqrt{5}\cdot\cos(\theta+\alpha)$, $\alpha=\tan^{-1}(\frac{1}{2})$ in line $4$. Note also $-1 \leqslant \cos (\theta+\alpha) \leqslant 1 \Rightarrow 0 \leqslant \cos ^{2}(\theta+\alpha) \leqslant 1 \Rightarrow 0 \leqslant T(x, y) \leqslant 125$. Hence, the highest temperature is $125$, and the lowest one is $0$.
